commit 67c340de4d02daf782e2e9069b7cf9b83a3df927
Author: Hao Ge <hao.ge@linux.dev>

mm/alloc_tag: clear codetag for pages allocated before page_ext initialization

This patch tracks early page allocations made before page_ext is initialized
by storing their PFNs in a fixed-size array. Once page_ext initialization
completes, the tracked pages have their codetags cleared to prevent warnings
when they are subsequently freed.

> diff --git a/lib/alloc_tag.c b/lib/alloc_tag.c
> index 58991ab09d84b..7b1812768af9a 100644
> --- a/lib/alloc_tag.c
> +++ b/lib/alloc_tag.c

[ ... ]

> +#define EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX		8192
> +
> +static unsigned long early_pfns[EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX] __initdata;
> +static atomic_t early_pfn_count __initdata = ATOMIC_INIT(0);

Is it safe to use a hardcoded limit of 8192 here? On systems with large core
counts or NUMA topologies, early SLAB fallbacks to the buddy allocator might
exceed this limit, causing the untracked pages to trigger the original
warning when freed.

Also, since these are marked __initdata, could a secondary CPU executing an
early allocation be delayed (e.g., by an interrupt) and write to early_pfns
after free_initmem() has already freed it, causing a use-after-free?

> +static void __init __alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn)
> +{
> +	int old_idx, new_idx;
> +
> +	do {
> +		old_idx = atomic_read(&early_pfn_count);
> +		if (old_idx >= EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX) {
> +			pr_warn_once("Early page allocations before page_ext init exceeded EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX (%d)\n",
> +				      EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX);
> +			return;
> +		}
> +		new_idx = old_idx + 1;
> +	} while (!atomic_try_cmpxchg(&early_pfn_count, &old_idx, new_idx));
> +
> +	early_pfns[old_idx] = pfn;
> +}

Could this lockless array insertion race with clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs?

The count is incremented via atomic_try_cmpxchg before the PFN is actually
written to early_pfns[old_idx]. If the cleanup loop reads early_pfn_count
right after the increment but before the write completes, it might read
uninitialized memory (or 0), missing the actual allocation entirely.

> +static void (*alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr)(unsigned long pfn) __refdata =
> +		__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn;

Similarly, __alloc_tag_add_early_pfn is an __init function. If a delayed
thread tries to call this pointer after boot completes, could it jump to
freed memory?

> +static void __init clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs(void)
> +{
> +	unsigned int i;
> +
> +	for (i = 0; i < atomic_read(&early_pfn_count); i++) {
> +		unsigned long pfn = early_pfns[i];

The page_ext_init function initializes memory sections sequentially. If an
early-allocated page from a newly initialized section is freed before
clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs runs at the end of the init callbacks,
__pgalloc_tag_sub will get a valid page_ext reference but find a NULL
codetag.

Will this still trigger the exact "alloc_tag was not set" warning this patch
intends to fix?

> diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
> index 192f7cbbe01d9..a35e715adde98 100644
> --- a/mm/page_alloc.c
> +++ b/mm/page_alloc.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -1257,6 +1257,13 @@ void __pgalloc_tag_add(struct page *page, struct task_struct *task,
>  		alloc_tag_add(&ref, task->alloc_tag, PAGE_SIZE * nr);
>  		update_page_tag_ref(handle, &ref);
>  		put_page_tag_ref(handle);
> +	} else {
> +		/*
> +		 * page_ext is not available yet, record the pfn so we can
> +		 * clear the tag ref later when page_ext is initialized.
> +		 */
> +		alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(page_to_pfn(page));

If nr > 1 (a high-order allocation), we only record the base PFN here.
If this allocation is later split before the tags are cleared, the tail
pages will not have their codetags updated. When those tail pages are later
freed, will they trigger the "alloc_tag was not set" warning?

> +		alloc_tag_set_inaccurate(current->alloc_tag);
>  	}
>  }

Is it safe to pass current->alloc_tag directly to alloc_tag_set_inaccurate
here?

For early boot allocations that use _noprof variants (such as kmemleak_alloc
falling back to the buddy allocator), current->alloc_tag is NULL outside of
alloc_hooks macro blocks.

Since alloc_tag_set_inaccurate unconditionally dereferences the tag to set a
flag, won't this cause a NULL pointer dereference panic during boot?

commit 67c340de4d02daf782e2e9069b7cf9b83a3df927
Author: Hao Ge <hao.ge@linux.dev>
Date:   Thu Mar 26 14:05:54 2026 +0000

    mm/alloc_tag: clear codetag for pages allocated before page_ext initialization
    
    Due to initialization ordering, page_ext is allocated and initialized
    relatively late during boot. Some pages have already been allocated
    and freed before page_ext becomes available, leaving their codetag
    uninitialized.
    
    A clear example is in init_section_page_ext(): alloc_page_ext() calls
    kmemleak_alloc(). If the slab cache has no free objects, it falls back
    to the buddy allocator to allocate memory. However, at this point page_ext
    is not yet fully initialized, so these newly allocated pages have no
    codetag set. These pages may later be reclaimed by KASAN, which causes
    the warning to trigger when they are freed because their codetag ref is
    still empty.
    
    Use a global array to track pages allocated before page_ext is fully
    initialized. The array size is fixed at 8192 entries, and will emit
    a warning if this limit is exceeded. When page_ext initialization
    completes, set their codetag to empty to avoid warnings when they
    are freed later.
    
    The following warning is observed when this issue occurs:

diff --git a/include/linux/alloc_tag.h b/include/linux/alloc_tag.h
index d40ac39bfbe8d..bf226c2be2ada 100644
--- a/include/linux/alloc_tag.h
+++ b/include/linux/alloc_tag.h
@@ -74,6 +74,8 @@ static inline void set_codetag_empty(union codetag_ref *ref)
 
 #ifdef CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING
 
+void alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn);
+
 #define ALLOC_TAG_SECTION_NAME	"alloc_tags"
 
 struct codetag_bytes {
diff --git a/include/linux/pgalloc_tag.h b/include/linux/pgalloc_tag.h
index 38a82d65e58e9..951d333622685 100644
--- a/include/linux/pgalloc_tag.h
+++ b/include/linux/pgalloc_tag.h
@@ -181,7 +181,7 @@ static inline struct alloc_tag *__pgalloc_tag_get(struct page *page)
 
 	if (get_page_tag_ref(page, &ref, &handle)) {
 		alloc_tag_sub_check(&ref);
-		if (ref.ct)
+		if (ref.ct && !is_codetag_empty(&ref))
 			tag = ct_to_alloc_tag(ref.ct);
 		put_page_tag_ref(handle);
 	}
diff --git a/lib/alloc_tag.c b/lib/alloc_tag.c
index 58991ab09d84b..7b1812768af9a 100644
--- a/lib/alloc_tag.c
+++ b/lib/alloc_tag.c
@@ -6,6 +6,7 @@
 #include <linux/kallsyms.h>
 #include <linux/module.h>
 #include <linux/page_ext.h>
+#include <linux/pgalloc_tag.h>
 #include <linux/proc_fs.h>
 #include <linux/seq_buf.h>
 #include <linux/seq_file.h>
@@ -26,6 +27,96 @@ static bool mem_profiling_support;
 
 static struct codetag_type *alloc_tag_cttype;
 
+#ifdef CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG
+
+/*
+ * Track page allocations before page_ext is initialized.
+ * Some pages are allocated before page_ext becomes available, leaving
+ * their codetag uninitialized. Track these early PFNs so we can clear
+ * their codetag refs later to avoid warnings when they are freed.
+ *
+ * Early allocations include:
+ *   - Base allocations independent of CPU count
+ *   - Per-CPU allocations (e.g., CPU hotplug callbacks during smp_init,
+ *     such as trace ring buffers, scheduler per-cpu data)
+ *
+ * For simplicity, we fix the size to 8192.
+ * If insufficient, a warning will be triggered to alert the user.
+ */
+#define EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX		8192
+
+static unsigned long early_pfns[EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX] __initdata;
+static atomic_t early_pfn_count __initdata = ATOMIC_INIT(0);
+
+static void __init __alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn)
+{
+	int old_idx, new_idx;
+
+	do {
+		old_idx = atomic_read(&early_pfn_count);
+		if (old_idx >= EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX) {
+			pr_warn_once("Early page allocations before page_ext init exceeded EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX (%d)\n",
+				      EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX);
+			return;
+		}
+		new_idx = old_idx + 1;
+	} while (!atomic_try_cmpxchg(&early_pfn_count, &old_idx, new_idx));
+
+	early_pfns[old_idx] = pfn;
+}
+
+static void (*alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr)(unsigned long pfn) __refdata =
+		__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn;
+
+void alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn)
+{
+	if (static_key_enabled(&mem_profiling_compressed))
+		return;
+
+	if (alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr)
+		alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr(pfn);
+}
+
+static void __init clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs(void)
+{
+	unsigned int i;
+
+	for (i = 0; i < atomic_read(&early_pfn_count); i++) {
+		unsigned long pfn = early_pfns[i];
+
+		if (pfn_valid(pfn)) {
+			struct page *page = pfn_to_page(pfn);
+			union pgtag_ref_handle handle;
+			union codetag_ref ref;
+
+			if (get_page_tag_ref(page, &ref, &handle)) {
+				/*
+				 * An early-allocated page could be freed and reallocated
+				 * after its page_ext is initialized but before we clear it.
+				 * In that case, it already has a valid tag set.
+				 * We should not overwrite that valid tag with CODETAG_EMPTY.
+				 */
+				if (ref.ct) {
+					put_page_tag_ref(handle);
+					continue;
+				}
+
+				set_codetag_empty(&ref);
+				update_page_tag_ref(handle, &ref);
+				put_page_tag_ref(handle);
+			}
+		}
+
+	}
+	atomic_set(&early_pfn_count, 0);
+
+	alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr = NULL;
+}
+#else /* !CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG */
+inline void alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn) {}
+static inline void __init clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs(void) {}
+#endif
+
 #ifdef CONFIG_ARCH_MODULE_NEEDS_WEAK_PER_CPU
 DEFINE_PER_CPU(struct alloc_tag_counters, _shared_alloc_tag);
 EXPORT_SYMBOL(_shared_alloc_tag);
@@ -760,6 +851,7 @@ static __init bool need_page_alloc_tagging(void)
 
 static __init void init_page_alloc_tagging(void)
 {
+	clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs();
 }
 
 struct page_ext_operations page_alloc_tagging_ops = {
diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
index 192f7cbbe01d9..a35e715adde98 100644
--- a/mm/page_alloc.c
+++ b/mm/page_alloc.c
@@ -1257,6 +1257,13 @@ void __pgalloc_tag_add(struct page *page, struct task_struct *task,
 		alloc_tag_add(&ref, task->alloc_tag, PAGE_SIZE * nr);
 		update_page_tag_ref(handle, &ref);
 		put_page_tag_ref(handle);
+	} else {
+		/*
+		 * page_ext is not available yet, record the pfn so we can
+		 * clear the tag ref later when page_ext is initialized.
+		 */
+		alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(page_to_pfn(page));
+		alloc_tag_set_inaccurate(current->alloc_tag);
 	}
 }

    "thought": "**A Deep Dive into the Early Page Allocation Tracking Patch**

Okay, here's the deal: this patch is trying to fix a race condition where the kernel's memory allocation tracking (`page_ext`) isn't initialized early enough during boot. Some pages get allocated *before* `page_ext` is ready, and that leads to warnings later when they're freed. The goal is to set up tags on pages allocated early, so the memory allocation tracking system doesn't generate erroneous warnings.

My initial thoughts? The author's approach is to use a fixed-size global array, `early_pfns`, to store the physical frame numbers (PFNs) of these early-allocated pages. They’ve got `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()` to add these PFNs to this array. Then, after `page_ext` is initialized, they clear these "early" tags, setting them to an empty state with `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs`. It seems sensible on the surface.

Now, things get interesting.  First, the fixed-size array (`EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX`) feels a bit like a potential bottleneck. Is 8192 a good size? That's only 32MB of pages.  During early boot, especially on systems with many CPUs or large memory, you could easily blow past that limit, especially when considering per-CPU allocations.  A fixed size seems fragile. If it *does* fill up, the patch just prints a warning and ignores further early allocations, which means untracked pages, and the original issue is back.  The original issue that the patch aims to solve!

I was digging into the logic and saw that if a page is *freed* before `page_ext` is initialized, the `__pgalloc_tag_sub` function would do *nothing*. This behavior is okay! The page is in the buddy system and ready to be reallocated. This is the behavior they want! When the page is reallocated, `page_ext` is initialized (which is to say, zeroed).

But there's an issue with the timing. When  `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` is executing, I questioned the thread-safety of populating this array!  The critical section is: read `early_pfn_count`, `cmpxchg`, write. I realized a subtle race is possible.  If a CPU’s `cmpxchg` succeeds, but before it can write the PFN to `early_pfns`, it gets interrupted by an IRQ. The IRQ could also add to the array. The race is subtle, but it's safe because `page_ext_init()` is called *before* SMP initialization.  The main thread is the *only* thread running at that point.  There could be IRQs, so they use the atomic `cmpxchg`, which makes it safe to run in an IRQ.

However, I started thinking deeply about the timing between `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` and IRQs. And I started to see problems.  While `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` runs, an IRQ might allocate a page and attempt to call `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()`.  This IRQ would succeed and add its PFN, but `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` only loops to the value of `atomic_read(&early_pfn_count)` *before* the loop.  So the IRQ PFN will not be processed, and it will be missed.

The timing of `page_ext_init()` matters.   It seems that at this point the memory management is *single* threaded and runs just after the initial setup.

Then I realized this patch introduced a potential for a critical NULL-pointer dereference.  If a page is allocated early and put in the `early_pfns` array, then freed before `page_ext` is fully initialized, it's codetag would be cleared by  `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()`. Now its codetag is set to a "special" empty value, `CODETAG_EMPTY`. *This is the critical bit.* If we have an allocation that happens in this very narrow window and the page is then reallocated. Then `__pgalloc_tag_add` will be called. Now `alloc_tag_add` is called, and a check `alloc_tag_add_check()` *doesn't* check for `CODETAG_EMPTY`. Thus it'll WARN, and it'll try to dereference a pointer at address 1, which will cause a crash.

Okay, so I'm diving into this `alloc_pages_exact` situation and trying to understand the implications for early boot processes. It seems like the core issue revolves around how we handle pages allocated before `page_ext_init()` versus those allocated after. I'm trying to figure out if there is a flaw in the current mechanism.

My initial thought was, could we use `__get_free_pages` early and then `free_page` some sub-pages later? The problem is that `split_page()` does exactly this. More generally, it appears that we are trying to manage pages that might not have the correct refcounting initialized. It looks like it is not allowed to `free_page()` a sub-page of a non-split, order-N page unless the caller manually handles the refcount splits. Also, if we use `split_page()` on early buddy allocations, the tail pages won't have the `early_pfns` tracking, which is part of the problem.

The architectural flaw is this fixed-size array (`early_pfns`). What happens when this 8192 array is fully populated? Well, we have this hardcoded limit, and if the array is full, we get a `pr_warn_once` and the PFN isn't added. Later, when the page is freed, `__pgalloc_tag_sub` calls `alloc_tag_sub_check`. Since `ref.ct == NULL`, we get a `WARN_ONCE`. So, if we exceed 8192 pages, we still get the warning the patch claims to fix. Is 32MB enough? Probably for *base* allocations, but what about large machines, with 256 cores? `kmem_cache_init()` might allocate many pages.

I started to think about what else happens before `page_ext_init()`: `kmemleak_init()`, `debug_objects_mem_init()`, `vfs_caches_init_early()`. Wait, the boot process is `mem_init()`, `kmem_cache_init()`, then `page_ext_init()`.

The issue becomes clearer when considering aggressive early debugging. SLUB debugging or KASAN might cause us to blow past that limit quickly. KASAN quarantine can consume up to 1/32 of memory. Actually, quarantine is populated when pages are *freed*, which is the root of the issue. The warning happens when KASAN reclaims those early pages. This means KASAN was enabled, and early pages were freed and went into the KASAN quarantine. This is why the patch is needed!

So, the fixed-size array is a definite limitation. How can we do this better?

If `page_ext` is allocated and initialized to zero, ALL pages will have `ref.ct == NULL` initially. After `page_ext_init`, when a page is allocated, it gets a valid codetag. The key is knowing if a page was allocated *before* the initialization. So, instead of tracking early allocations, why don't we just initialize `ref.ct = CODETAG_EMPTY` for ALL pages during `page_ext_init()` if they are already allocated?         page = pfn_to_page(pfn);
        if (!PageBuddy(page)) {
            /* Set CODETAG_EMPTY */
        }
    }
}
```

Okay, here's what I've found after carefully dissecting this page allocation tagging patch. It seems well-intentioned, but there are a few critical flaws that need to be addressed before it's ready for prime time.

First, I'm deeply concerned about the **incomplete tracking of high-order allocations**. The patch's current implementation, which relies on `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn`, only captures the *base* page frame number (PFN) of an early allocation in the `early_pfns` array. It completely ignores the *order* of the allocation. Now, the problem arises when this high-order page is later split (e.g., via `split_page()`) or if it's freed page-by-page. Because the patch *only* clears the tag on the base page, all the *tail* pages of the original allocation end up with a `NULL` codetag. When these sub-pages are later freed individually, the system will incorrectly trigger the `alloc_tag was not set` warning, which is precisely what the patch is *trying* to avoid. The author's assumption is, fundamentally, incorrect in that splitting an allocation is a perfectly valid scenario, as is freeing sub-pages of a large, non-compound allocation.

Second, the use of a **fixed-size array (`EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX`) is a major anti-pattern**. A static array of 8192 entries simply isn't robust enough for tracking early allocations across a diverse range of systems. This approach creates a ticking time bomb. The number of memory allocations made between the buddy allocator and `page_ext_init()` – the window this patch targets – grows with system size, NUMA nodes, CPU count, and especially with the use of memory debugging features like KASAN, SLUB debugging, etc. This array will be filled far sooner on larger machines, or even with the use of debug tools. When the array overflows, the system silently drops tracking for these early pages. The consequences? They'll trigger the exact same `alloc_tag was not set` warning later when they are freed. This defeats the purpose of the patch. The allocation tracker has to scale with the kernel size.

Finally, and this is a subtle but serious problem, there's a **race condition within `page_ext_init()`**. The `page_ext_init()` function initializes zone-by-zone, but crucially, it defers calling `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` (which sets the codetags) to a later callback through `invoke_init_callbacks()`. The time window between the zone initialization and `invoke_init_callbacks` is a race condition. It's during this window that the system will falsely trigger the warning. If, during that window, an early-allocated page is *freed* – perhaps through temporary allocations within `alloc_page_ext()`, or through operations within a mempool (like `kmemleak_alloc`) – the `__pgalloc_tag_sub` function will see the still-uninitialized `page_ext` and incorrectly trigger the warning because the codetag will still be `NULL`. This scenario reveals a race and a fundamental flaw in the patch's design.

To summarize the flaws:
- Incomplete code for high-order allocations that can get split
- Fixed size data structure for an unbounded problem.
- Race window within page_ext_init.

I am confident in these findings.

Alright, after meticulously tracing the code, I've identified a critical NULL pointer dereference and a race condition within the memory profiling subsystem, specifically in the `alloc_tag` implementation. Here's a breakdown of my thought process and findings:

This function is responsible for tagging memory allocations, especially those happening early in the boot process. However, a significant flaw exists: `current->alloc_tag` can be NULL! This happens when an allocation occurs via `_noprof` variants like `kmem_cache_alloc_noprof` or `alloc_pages_noprof`, bypassing the `alloc_hooks` macro. The `alloc_hooks` macro is crucial because it's what sets `current->alloc_tag`. For allocations in functions like `kmemleak_alloc`, `current->alloc_tag` remains NULL.  Since `alloc_tag_set_inaccurate` lacks a null check, dereferencing `NULL` causes a guaranteed kernel crash. This is a 100% reproducible NULL pointer dereference.

The second, albeit less severe, issue is a concurrency race during early page allocation tracking, specifically a potential TOCTOU (Time-of-Check-to-Time-of-Use) problem.  `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` is responsible for tracking early allocations before the `page_ext` data structure is fully initialized. The problem is that it *increments* `early_pfn_count` using `atomic_try_cmpxchg` *before* it actually writes the PFN to the `early_pfns` array.  Concurrently, another CPU might be running `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` (which is guaranteed when `CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT` is enabled). The other CPU will read the *incremented* count and, before the PFN is stored, it will read an uninitialized/zero value from the `early_pfns` array. This results in the tag of the intended PFN not being cleared and can result in `pfn_valid(0)` to be triggered which modifies PFN 0 if it exists. Moreover, PFNs added right after the loop in `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` finishes will be missed completely, as the associated pointer is set to NULL at the end.

Furthermore, it is important to point out that even if `pfn_valid(0)` is false, it can still incorrectly skip an PFN, which is a serious issue.

We have an atomic counter, `early_pfn_count`, tracking the number of early allocations. `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` adds a PFN to the `early_pfns` array, using an atomic compare-and-swap (`atomic_try_cmpxchg`) to handle concurrent access.  `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` iterates through the array, retrieves each PFN, and attempts to clear its codetag. A check for a valid PFN is in place, and if the page already has a valid tag, it's skipped. Smart, that prevents overwriting a legitimate tag.

But here's where my expert instincts kick in. That `atomic_try_cmpxchg` in `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn`... if it fails, the loop retries. It seems safe as the `old_idx` is updated, but if that `old_idx` is already `EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX` then it will warn and return. That is safe.

Then there is the issue of concurrency. Is the read and write to `early_pfns[old_idx]` atomic?

Aha, the real concern is the concurrent execution. Let's imagine: an allocation happens concurrently with the clearing process. If a thread gets preempted after the `atomic_try_cmpxchg` succeeds, but *before* writing the PFN to `early_pfns`, that creates a race condition. `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` might read the *incremented* count, but will not find the newly added PFN yet. It will use a `0`. This is the crux. It'll process the bogus index, and then the delayed thread will update the array, but it's too late. The `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` has already passed over it. Then, `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` finishes. The thread will free the page later, and we get the KASAN warning.

And the call of `get_page_tag_ref`. That suggests that this function is called when the `page_ext` is not available, because it returns false.

Where is `page_ext_init()` called? That's the critical question. If it's called during early boot, and SMP is active... bingo. Concurrent allocations *are* possible, particularly if the initialisation is not completed in all NUMA nodes. This is a critical race condition.

I need to confirm if `page_ext_init` and therefore, `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` happens after `smp_init`, so that the above scenario can occur. And yes, it does!

So, to summarize the race:
Thread 1:
- `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn`
- `atomic_read(&early_pfn_count)`
- `atomic_try_cmpxchg(&early_pfn_count)` succeeds, increments count.
- Gets preempted.

Thread 2:
- `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` runs.
- Reads `early_pfn_count` (which has been incremented by the previous thread).
- Iterates to process the PFN.
- Reads `early_pfns[new_idx -1]`. It's the old value (0) because it hasn't been written to.
- Sets `atomic_set(&early_pfn_count, 0)`.
- Sets `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr = NULL`.

Thread 1:
- Resumes.
- `early_pfns[old_idx] = pfn;` <--- Writes the value.

Thread 2 has already completed, and it never processed this page, meaning that it is now lost. KASAN will trigger on free. I need to get this flagged.

Okay, so I've been tracing the memory allocation path, specifically around the initialization of page extensions and the handling of early page allocations. I've found a critical race condition. It seems like the kernel's initialization sequence creates a window where a Use-After-Free (UAF) bug can occur, potentially leading to a system crash.

Here's the problem: `page_alloc_init_late()` is called after SMP is up and running, meaning other CPUs can be concurrently allocating memory. This function has a critical wait on `pgdat_init_all_done_comp` *before* initializing page extensions and calling `page_ext_init()`. The core of the issue is the interaction between `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` and `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs`.

The `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` function is designed to handle memory allocations that occur *before* the page extension is fully initialized. When `page_ext` isn't available, this function records the PFN (page frame number) to be tagged later. The problem lies in the fact that concurrent operations with `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` can lead to a race. `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` is responsible for clearing the early allocation PFNs after `page_ext` is fully initialized, and it writes `atomic_set(&early_pfn_count, 0)` and `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr = NULL` in an unsynchronized manner. This creates a classic time-of-check-to-time-of-use (TOCTOU) race.

Here's the scenario: Imagine one CPU (CPU B) is inside `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` while another CPU (CPU A) is executing `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs`. CPU B reads an index into the `early_pfns` array, then attempts to update the `early_pfn_count`. At this point, CPU A might have already read a low value of `early_pfn_count` and is iterating through the array. CPU B *successfully* increments `early_pfn_count` and starts writing to `early_pfns`. However, CPU A's loop is already past this point and won't see the new entry. Then, CPU A sets `early_pfn_count` to zero, and `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr = NULL`.

The key vulnerability is that after this operation, a page that CPU B allocated might *never* have its tag cleared! Even worse, `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr` is then set to NULL.

The real kicker comes with the fact that `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` is called via a function pointer, `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr`.  Because the system is fully up in SMP, the `init` text is likely to be freed via `free_initmem()` soon after `page_alloc_init_late()` has run. If CPU B reads the non-NULL `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr` value, then gets preempted. CPU A runs `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` and frees the `__init` memory, and sets `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr = NULL`. Then CPU B resumes and calls a function pointer in deallocated `__init` memory; this can lead to an execution-after-free crash (UAF). The race exists in `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` itself, as it calls `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr`, a function pointer to `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn`. This is a classic TOCTOU race.

Furthermore, `early_pfns` is marked `__initdata`, which means the array itself is freed, and the crash happens when we access a freed array.

The window of vulnerability is the time between when SMP starts, and when `free_initmem()` is called, relatively small.  `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` is called from `__pgalloc_tag_add` during memory allocation, and this is called from `post_alloc_hook` which might be preemptible, and this race can definitely occur.

First, and this is the big one, we have a **Use-After-Free** or, in this case, a likely **Execution-After-Free** vulnerability. The code checks a function pointer, `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr`, and if it's not NULL, it *calls* the function it points to. The catch? There's no protection around that read-and-call sequence. A thread can be preempted right after that check. It doesn’t matter if the compiler reloads the pointer twice or once; preemption, interruptions, and context switches are all possible at that exact moment. Now, the `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` function – which runs during early initialization – *also* sets this function pointer to NULL. So, the preemption window is open, the initialization continues and finishes, `__init` memory is freed, and the first thread eventually resumes. At this point, it happily jumps to the now-freed memory to execute its function pointer, which means a kernel crash.

Then we have a race condition that will cause pages to be *missed*. This `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` records early page allocations. It uses an atomic counter, `early_pfn_count`, and an array, `early_pfns`, to store the PFNs. The issue is that the counter is incremented *before* the PFN is actually written to the array. If the `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` function runs *concurrently* (and remember, preemption is enabled), it will see the *incremented* count, so it will iterate up to the new value in the array. However, because the writing thread hasn't actually written the PFN yet, `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` will read uninitialized memory (likely 0, depending on the architecture). This function will process the uninitialized value as a PFN that needs its tag cleared and then finish, and when the original function tries to write its PFN in the array, it’s too late. The PFN's codetag is now completely missed. Later, when that page is freed, KASAN will rightfully complain because the codetag hasn't been set. The race can happen in many scenarios, and the function pointer can be set to NULL after the loop too, causing a missed PFN. There is no synchronization. And it’s not just a race on a single memory location; it's a race between threads and the clearing of a tag. The second race can cause the tag clearing of PFN 0 if `pfn_valid(0)` is true.

This brings up important questions. Are there other problems? No, the maximum number is bounded. Is the locking correct? No. The main problem is that `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` is called from page allocator, so preemption is definitely enabled.

So, in summary: we have two major concurrency problems here.

    "thought": "**Race Condition in Early Page Allocation with Deferred `page_ext_init`**

Okay, so I'm looking at `mm/page_ext.c` and how `page_ext_init()` gets called. It's either from `mm_core_init()` or, when `CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT` is enabled, from `deferred_init_memmap()`. The critical thought here is that with the deferred initialization, `page_ext_init` runs *late*, like after secondary CPUs are up. That got me thinking... wait a minute, could memory allocation happen on those secondary CPUs *before* `page_ext_init` runs in the deferred scenario? 

No, that's not possible; the deferred init happens in kthreads created during `rest_init`, and `rest_init` brings up CPUs via `kernel_init` -> `smp_init()`.

But hold on, this led me to the real problem: if early allocations happen concurrently with the execution of `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()`, we could have a race. That function is called, via `invoke_init_callbacks`, from `page_ext_init` itself, and it looks like this:

```c
static void __init clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs(void)
{
	unsigned int i;

	for (i = 0; i < atomic_read(&early_pfn_count); i++) {
		unsigned long pfn = early_pfns[i];
...
	}
	atomic_set(&early_pfn_count, 0);

	alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr = NULL;
}
```

Now, imagine a CPU calling `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()`:

```c
	do {
		old_idx = atomic_read(&early_pfn_count);
		if (old_idx >= EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX) {
			pr_warn_once("Early page allocations before page_ext init exceeded EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX (%d)\n",
				      EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX);
			return;
		}
		new_idx = old_idx + 1;
	} while (!atomic_try_cmpxchg(&early_pfn_count, &old_idx, new_idx));

	early_pfns[old_idx] = pfn;
```

Here's where the race happens:
1.  An allocating CPU increments `early_pfn_count` but hasn't yet written the PFN value to `early_pfns[old_idx]`.

    "thought": "**Race Condition Detected in Early Page Allocation Tagging**

Alright, let's break this down. I've pinpointed a potential race condition in the early initialization of page allocation tagging, specifically around `page_alloc_init_late` and its interaction with multiple CPUs during system boot. `page_alloc_init_late` is called after `smp_init`, meaning secondary CPUs are active and potentially allocating memory concurrently. This concurrency is where the trouble starts.

The core issue lies in the `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` function, which is designed to handle early page allocations before `page_ext` is fully initialized.  It uses an atomic compare-and-swap (`atomic_try_cmpxchg`) to update a counter (`early_pfn_count`) and an array (`early_pfns`).  The problem is, the increment of `early_pfn_count` happens *before* the corresponding PFN is written to the array. This creates a classic "reserve then write" scenario, a known source of race conditions.

Consider this: CPU A successfully increments `early_pfn_count` and is then interrupted *before* writing the PFN to `early_pfns`. Meanwhile, CPU B runs `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()`, which iterates through `early_pfns` up to the value of the *incremented* count, but reads 0 at the not-yet-written index. It then *falsely* attempts to clear the codetag ref for a non-existent allocation because it's reading the uninitialized value of `early_pfns[old_idx]`. When CPU A resumes, it writes the actual PFN, but it's now "lost" because `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` has already finished.

The impact is severe: when that page is later freed, KASAN (or similar tools) will complain because the codetag wasn't properly initialized, defeating the entire purpose of the patch. The use of a simple spinlock to protect the `early_pfns` access would have been trivial, but now I must address this.

Beyond the core race, I looked at a few other issues. The sentinel value doesn't protect against concurrent preemption between the writer and the reader, as both could be on the same CPU!

Another race:
1.  If the page is freed and reallocated
   - If a page allocated early is freed *after* `page_ext` is initialized but *before* `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` clears its tag: `__pgalloc_tag_sub` calls `alloc_tag_sub(&ref, PAGE_SIZE * nr);`.
   - Wait, if `ref->ct` is NULL (because the tag hasn't been cleared to `CODETAG_EMPTY` yet, wait no, `ref.ct` would be NULL because `page_ext` memory is zero-initialized).
   - Wait, if `page_ext` memory is zero-initialized, then `ref.ct` is NULL.
   - If `ref.ct` is NULL, `alloc_tag_sub` just returns!
   - Wait, but the bug report says:
   - ```
   - alloc_tag was not set
   - WARNING: ./include/linux/alloc_tag.h:164 at __pgalloc_tag_sub+0x40f/0x550, CPU#5: systemd/1
   - ```
   - Let's check `alloc_tag_sub_check(ref)`.

The fix: A simple spinlock (e.g., `DEFINE_SPINLOCK(early_pfn_lock); spin_lock(&early_pfn_lock); ... spin_unlock(&early_pfn_lock);`) would completely prevent this, especially since this is an early boot, low-contention slow-path (fallback before `page_ext` is initialized).

This requires a careful fix, the introduction of a spinlock is a must!

Okay, so I'm diving into this `lookup_page_ext` function, and I see it uses `READ_ONCE(section->page_ext)`. The key here is that once this `page_ext` is set, `get_page_tag_ref` starts returning `TRUE`. Let's visualize this as a CPU executing `page_ext_init()` for sparsemem. It allocates the memory and writes to `section->page_ext`. Boom, at *this* precise moment, `get_page_tag_ref()` starts returning `TRUE` for every page within that section, and it continues this way until the end.

`page_ext_init` then progresses to initializing the remaining sections, and then finally calls `invoke_init_callbacks()`, which in turn, calls `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()`. Here's where it gets interesting. If an early-allocated page in the *first* section is freed *after* its `section->page_ext` is set but *before* `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` runs, `__pgalloc_tag_sub` is going to see `get_page_tag_ref == true`, read `ref.ct` (which is going to be `NULL` because the `page_ext` allocation was zero-initialized and `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` hasn't run yet), and trigger that nasty `WARNING: alloc_tag was not set`!

This means that the patch we're looking at isn't *completely* fixing the issue. We've just got a smaller window for the bug to happen. If a page was allocated early, we know that its `pfn` is stored in `early_pfns`. Later on, `page_ext_init()` runs and it populates the `section->page_ext`. Then, *right* after the population, an interrupt or another CPU can free that early page. `__pgalloc_tag_sub` calls `get_page_tag_ref` and, since `section->page_ext` is populated, it returns `true`. Then `__pgalloc_tag_sub` calls `alloc_tag_sub`, and that function calls `alloc_tag_sub_check(ref)`, which, seeing `!ref->ct` and WARNS!

This is a totally plausible race, because `page_ext_init()` fills `page_ext` sequentially and only calls `invoke_init_callbacks()` at the very end. The timeframe between `section->page_ext` being set and `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` being called is relatively huge.

So, to actually fix this, we need `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` to run *before* `page_ext_init` is done, but we're stuck because we can only control the execution order of `page_ext_init` via a callback. But hold on... the fix the author has implemented *is* clearing the tags in `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs`. But wait, during `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()`, if `get_page_tag_ref()` fails, it does nothing. So it relies on `page_ext` being initialized! And we can't clear the tags immediately when `section->page_ext` is allocated because the `page_ext` is a generic mechanism.

	union pgtag_ref_handle handle;

The problem is that a concurrent reader, `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs`, reads `early_pfn_count` and iterates up to that value. Because the writer's write to `early_pfns[old_idx]` hasn't happened yet (or may not have happened on another CPU), the reader can end up accessing uninitialized memory at `early_pfns[old_idx]`. This means the reader can see a zero-initialized value (due to `__initdata`), and consequently try to clear the code tag of the *wrong* page (PFN 0), while the real page that was allocated is not cleared. Since the code tag is not cleared, the intended fix will never be completed. This is a classic concurrency bug, and the easiest fix here, given the early boot, low-contention nature of this code, is to just use a spinlock to protect both the increment of the count and the array assignment.

Second, I see a potential **window for spurious warnings** due to the timing between when the `page_ext` structures are allocated and when the early page tags are cleared. `page_ext_init` allocates the `page_ext` structures per section and *then* calls `invoke_init_callbacks()`, which *eventually* runs `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs`. The issue is, any early allocations freed during the time between when the `page_ext` is assigned (for a particular section) and when `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` runs are at risk.     "thought": "**Analyzing the Early Page Allocation Tagging Issue**

Okay, so I'm looking at how page tags are handled during early allocation, and I see a potential problem. `pgalloc_tag_split` seems straightforward – it grabs the tag from the base page, using `__pgalloc_tag_get`, and then copies it to the split pages, *but only if the tag is not NULL*. If it's an early allocation where the `page_ext` isn't set up yet, `__pgalloc_tag_get` returns `NULL`. That's important; it *doesn't* copy `CODETAG_EMPTY` or anything like that.

Then, `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` is supposed to clean up, but it only clears `page_ext` for the *base* PFN, not the split pages. This means the split pages can end up with uninitialized `page_ext` data. When these pages are freed later, the `__pgalloc_tag_sub` function then triggers a warning because `ref.ct` is `NULL`. Is this a valid scenario? Yes, high-order pages are possible, the buddy allocator can return them before `page_ext` is fully initialized, and they can be split. The concern is that if the early allocation does not copy the `CODETAG_EMPTY` tag, then it would stay `NULL`. `__pgalloc_tag_sub` will warn if `ref.ct == NULL`. 

There's another issue here: **the fixed array limit.** If `EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX` is exceeded, we give up tracking. That's a hard limit. At 8192 pages at 4KB each, that's only 32MB. On large systems, it's possible to exceed this limit (per-CPU allocations, etc.). It prints a warning if exceeded, and then it stops tracking. This is a real limitation, and it's flagged as a high-severity issue.

**Finally, the lockless array insertion is a major problem, too!**
The way the code adds PFNS to `early_pfns` is using this reserve-then-write pattern, which is NOT safe for concurrent readers. It reserves an index and then writes to it, but it's not atomic. If `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` is running concurrently, it might read an uninitialized array element. If a writer increments `early_pfn_count` from 5 to 6, then `atomic_read(&early_pfn_count)` in the reader will be 6. The reader will read `early_pfns[5]`. But the writer hasn't executed `early_pfns[5] = pfn;` yet! This means the reader can see a zero-initialized value (since `early_pfns` is `__initdata` and is zero-initialized), which gets treated as a valid page, but it's not the actual page. It then tries to modify `page_ext` on PFN 0! This is a recipe for memory corruption, even if pfn 0 is often valid. This is a severe problem. The `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` runs concurrently. This function is called from `init_page_alloc_tagging()`. I need to track down where *that* function is called.

Okay, so I'm looking at this `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` function, and I'm starting to see a serious issue here. It's run right at the tail end of `page_ext_init()`, after this long process of section iteration. During `page_ext_init()`, we have `init_section_page_ext()` which calls `alloc_page_ext()`. Now, `alloc_page_ext()` calls `kmemleak_alloc()`, and that's where the problem kicks off.

Essentially, `kmemleak_alloc()` can end up allocating memory using the buddy allocator *during* the `page_ext_init()` phase. This is concerning because `page_ext` isn't fully initialized at this point. The core of the problem lies in the concurrency. `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` is iterating through a list of early allocations, represented by `early_pfns[]`, and it's doing this *after* allocations can potentially happen within `page_ext_init()`.

Here's the sequence that's giving me pause: `kmemleak_alloc()` can get called, which, if the slab cache is empty, uses the buddy allocator to allocate. When an allocation happens it uses `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()` to track it. Concurrently, `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` is iterating through the `early_pfns[]` array. Here's where the race begins.

Imagine a scenario: the `atomic_try_cmpxchg` operation reserves an index and increments the count, but `early_pfns[old_idx] = pfn` hasn't been written to the array yet. `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` then reads the incremented `early_pfn_count` and proceeds with the loop. It reads `early_pfns[old_idx] == 0`. It then calls `set_codetag_empty()` for PFN 0. Then, the code calls `atomic_set(&early_pfn_count, 0)` and `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr = NULL`. The allocation, which just incremented the count, might have just written to `early_pfns[old_idx]`, but that memory is now completely ignored, and its tag is never cleared. This, of course, will trigger a warning later.

Even worse, consider the scenario where an allocation happens *after* `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` reads the count, but *before* the code sets `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr = NULL`. The allocation will write to `early_pfns[old_idx]`, but because the loop is already finished, this new early PFN is ignored. And then the count is reset! This means that allocated pages are never tagged or cleaned up, potentially leading to future leaks or incorrect behavior.

The core question here is whether `page_ext_init()` can run concurrently. I'm going to have to dig into its callers to be sure, but it feels like the race condition is very real and very serious. We need to prevent the iteration process from racing with any potential allocation processes.

Okay, so I'm digging into the kernel initialization sequence, specifically around memory management. I'm looking at `mm/mm_init.c` and immediately see a couple of calls to `page_ext_init()`. The first one is conditionally executed if `deferred_struct_pages` is set, right after all the struct pages are initialized. Interesting. But then there's *another* call to `page_ext_init()` later, this time *only* if `deferred_struct_pages` is *not* set. That's a little… redundant, isn't it? Seems odd to have two calls with mutually exclusive conditions. Both of those calls are nestled within `mm_core_init()`.

Ah, right, `mm_core_init()` is called from `start_kernel()`. Okay, that's the big boot-up function. But here's where my mind starts racing: where does `smp_init()` fit into this picture? Is it *before* `mm_core_init()`? Or *after*? The order of these initialization steps is crucial. One leads to another, dependencies... I need to go back to `start_kernel()` in `init/main.c` and figure out the exact call sequence. I need to understand if `page_ext_init()` is called, under the conditional, before the SMP setup, or if SMP has to be running first. Knowing the timing of `smp_init()` is going to be key to understanding the purpose of these calls.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Concurrent Memory Initialization and Tagging**

Okay, let's break this down. First, I see that `smp_init()` is called after `mm_core_init()` but before `page_alloc_init_late()` in the kernel initialization sequence. Crucially, if `deferred_struct_pages` is set, `page_ext_init()` is called within `page_alloc_init_late()`. This means that during the execution of `page_alloc_init_late()`, secondary CPUs are already online and potentially allocating memory concurrently.

Now, during `page_alloc_init_late()`, `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` is executed. This is where things get interesting. Secondary CPUs could be calling `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()` *concurrently*. That function uses `atomic_try_cmpxchg` to atomically reserve an index in the `early_pfns` array, but importantly, it writes to `early_pfns[old_idx]` *afterwards* without any locks. This is a red flag. I see it as five distinct race conditions.

Firstly, if `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` runs concurrently with `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` and reads 0 as count, it will clear the tag for PFN 0 when it should be clearing a later entry.

Secondly, Interrupts allocating memory during this `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` call will have their allocated PFNs permanently lost.

Thirdly, `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` uses a lockless array insertion mechanism. This can lead to race conditions where concurrent readers see uninitialized array elements.

Fourthly, this is a classic UAF write! The `early_pfns` array resides in the `__initdata` section. If a secondary CPU gets stalled between successfully reserving an index with `atomic_try_cmpxchg` and writing the PFN to the array, and then the boot CPU calls `free_initmem()`, then the stalled CPU will write to freed memory when it eventually resumes. This is a **Critical** issue.

Let's trace that scenario in detail.  A secondary CPU in `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()` increments `early_pfn_count`, gets an index, but then is preempted or delayed, perhaps by a hypervisor. The boot CPU continues, finishes `start_kernel()`, enters `kernel_init()`, and eventually calls `free_initmem()`, reclaiming the `__initdata` section. If a secondary CPU then resumes and attempts to write the PFN to `early_pfns[old_idx]`, it will be writing to freed memory. This is a textbook UAF write. Critical.

Therefore, the concurrency/race issues can be grouped into two main findings:
1.  **Data Race / Concurrency Bug in Early Tracking Array**:  The `atomic_try_cmpxchg` loop reserves an index before the PFN is written. Concurrent reads (e.g., `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` running on another CPU during `page_alloc_init_late`) can see uninitialized memory. Allocations happening after a reader caches `atomic_read(&early_pfn_count)` but before the function pointer is cleared are permanently lost, causing spurious warnings during freeing. (Severity: High)
2.  **Use-After-Free Write / Execution-After-Free due to lack of synchronization**: `early_pfns` and `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` are in `__initdata` and `__init` memory. Because the function is lockless, a thread on a secondary CPU can be preempted or stalled between reserving the index and writing to the array. If the boot process continues and `free_initmem()` reclaims the memory, the delayed thread will overwrite active kernel memory when it resumes.  (Severity: Critical)

So, if we bypass the standard `alloc_hooks()` path and call `alloc_pages_noprof()` directly, `current->alloc_tag` will remain unchanged from its previous value. This means it could be `NULL` if we weren't already inside of a profiling-enabled allocation. Most allocations are now wrapped with `alloc_hooks()` via `alloc_pages()`, which *does* use the hooks, but direct calls to `alloc_pages_noprof()` bypass that.

A scenario where we'd see `current->alloc_tag` as `NULL` is if code deliberately calls `alloc_pages_noprof()` even though memory allocation profiling is enabled via the boot parameter `mem_alloc_profiling_enabled()`. In this case, `current->alloc_tag` would be `NULL`, and that's concerning.

My immediate question is whether `alloc_tag_set_inaccurate(NULL)` will crash the kernel. The code for this function is straightforward: it sets a flag on the `alloc_tag` structure, dereferencing a pointer to `NULL`.

static inline void alloc_tag_set_inaccurate(struct alloc_tag *tag)
{
	tag->ct.flags |= CODETAG_FLAG_INACCURATE;
}
```

This dereference looks like a recipe for a kernel panic if `tag` is indeed `NULL`.

However, `alloc_tag_add` itself *does* have a check:

```c
static inline void alloc_tag_add_check(union codetag_ref *ref, struct alloc_tag *tag)
{
	WARN_ONCE(ref && ref->ct && !is_codetag_empty(ref), ...);
	WARN_ONCE(!tag, "current->alloc_tag not set\n");
}
static inline bool __alloc_tag_ref_set(union codetag_ref *ref, struct alloc_tag *tag)
{

Even if we aren't using `_noprof`, there might be allocations during early boot before `alloc_tag_sec_init`, but `mem_alloc_profiling_enabled` is checked.

In summary, `current->alloc_tag` can be `NULL` if a function like `alloc_pages_noprof()` is called, OR if `pgalloc_tag_add` is called when no tag is recorded. Since `alloc_tag_set_inaccurate` doesn't check for a `NULL` tag, this situation will cause a kernel panic! I need to verify that.

Okay, so I've been digging into this allocation tagging code, and I've found a few pretty nasty issues. It seems like the core issue is around handling allocations made before `page_ext` is fully initialized, especially those that bypass the standard allocation wrappers.

First, this unconditional dereference of `current->alloc_tag` in `alloc_tag_set_inaccurate` when `page_ext` isn't ready is a showstopper. It results in a NULL pointer dereference in the `_noprof` early allocations, which can happen in kernel threads. This is **Critical** severity, no question.

Second, that `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` function is ripe for Use-After-Free and Execution-After-Free issues. Using the pointer `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr` without proper synchronization and in `__initdata` memory is asking for trouble. If a secondary CPU gets preempted during that operation and `free_initmem` runs, boom - arbitrary write or jump to freed memory. Another **Critical** bug.

Third, I'm concerned about a data race in `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` and the `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` function. The atomic increment with `atomic_try_cmpxchg` for `early_pfn_count` before the PFN write means `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` can read a stale, uninitialized PFN. This results in missed early PFNs, which means KASAN warnings that the patch is meant to fix are actually triggered when pages are freed. That's **High** severity.

The fourth issue is the fixed-size array (`EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX`). 8192 is not a lot and the tracking silently fails if this limit is exceeded, leaving the original warning in place. Another **High** severity issue.

Fifth, there are spurious warnings during `page_ext_init`. If a page gets freed while that module is running, `__pgalloc_tag_sub` can find the page extension but still get a null codetag because `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` hasn't run yet. **High** severity.

Lastly, there is an incomplete tracking of high-order pages. If `alloc_pages(order=3)` is called while `page_ext` is not ready, only the base page's PFN is recorded. Later, if the page is split, the tag won't propagate, and the tail pages will get a NULL codetag, leading to the warning the patch is trying to fix.

Okay, after a deep dive, I've got a solid understanding of these tag allocation issues. It seems we have a series of related, but distinct, problems stemming from the early allocation and initialization of page tags. My primary concern is that a lot of this hinges on very subtle timing issues during the boot process and that there are several high-severity vulnerabilities in the code, and a couple of critical ones.

First, I see a classic race condition in the `__alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` function. The use of `atomic_try_cmpxchg` to reserve an index before actually writing the PFN opens a window for concurrent readers to observe uninitialized memory. With `CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT`, and the fact that `page_ext_init` runs concurrently, it's possible for secondary CPUs to allocate memory while the cleanup process is running. This leads to a reader potentially finding a `0` value in the `early_pfns` array, falsely clearing a codetag and potentially missing an actual early allocation entirely. Furthermore, allocations occurring after the count is read but before the PFN is written can be silently discarded and their references uninitialized. That's a high-severity issue.

Second, the fact that `early_pfns` and related functions reside in `__init` memory is a massive red flag. A delayed thread executing code after `free_initmem()` reclaims that memory is essentially set up for a Use-After-Free scenario. A secondary CPU could be preempted before writing, and after `kernel_init` calls `free_initmem()`, the delayed thread could jump to a freed address or perform a write into active kernel memory. This is critical.

Third, the unconditional dereference in `alloc_tag_set_inaccurate()` is just plain bad. Since many early kernel allocations bypass the alloc hooks, `current->alloc_tag` can be `NULL` in the context of these `_noprof` allocations. A blind write without a NULL check results in a kernel panic and that's critical.

Fourth, the hardcoded `EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX` limit is a disaster waiting to happen on large configurations. Systems with high core counts, or specific boot parameters which might trigger more early buddy allocations, can easily exceed this limit. Once exceeded, we drop tracking. This means that when those untracked pages are later freed, their `page_ext` codetags will be uninitialized, leading to a predictable warning. High severity.

Fifth, the patch doesn't handle high-order allocations properly. `__pgalloc_tag_add` only stores the base PFN for these allocations. If an early high-order page is split before `page_ext` initialization, `pgalloc_tag_split()` returns without touching the tail pages.  `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` only sets the base PFN, and when the tail pages are freed, their uninitialized tags will trigger the warning. Again, this is high-severity.

Finally, we have spurious warnings that can pop up during the `page_ext_init` window.  `page_ext_init()` is performed in sections, and while a section is being populated, `get_page_tag_ref()` will return true for pages in that section. But, `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` doesn't get called until the *end* of the process. If an early allocated page from an already populated section is freed before the clean-up loop runs, `__pgalloc_tag_sub` will find the codetag as still `NULL` causing a false warning. High severity.

We have a clear set of problems here. And the severity is High and Critical.

    "content": "commit 67c340de4d02daf782e2e9069b7cf9b83a3df927
Author: Hao Ge <hao.ge@linux.dev>

mm/alloc_tag: clear codetag for pages allocated before page_ext initialization

This patch tracks early page allocations made before page_ext is initialized
by storing their PFNs in a fixed-size array. Once page_ext initialization
completes, the tracked pages have their codetags cleared to prevent warnings
when they are subsequently freed.

> diff --git a/lib/alloc_tag.c b/lib/alloc_tag.c
> index 58991ab09d84b..7b1812768af9a 100644
> --- a/lib/alloc_tag.c
> +++ b/lib/alloc_tag.c

[ ... ]

> +#define EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX		8192
> +
> +static unsigned long early_pfns[EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX] __initdata;
> +static atomic_t early_pfn_count __initdata = ATOMIC_INIT(0);

Is it safe to use a hardcoded limit of 8192 here? On systems with large core
counts or NUMA topologies, early SLAB fallbacks to the buddy allocator might
exceed this limit, causing the untracked pages to trigger the original
warning when freed.

Also, since these are marked __initdata, could a secondary CPU executing an
early allocation be delayed (e.g., by an interrupt) and write to early_pfns
after free_initmem() has already freed it, causing a use-after-free?

> +static void __init __alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn)
> +{
> +	int old_idx, new_idx;
> +
> +	do {
> +		old_idx = atomic_read(&early_pfn_count);
> +		if (old_idx >= EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX) {
> +			pr_warn_once("Early page allocations before page_ext init exceeded EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX (%d)\n",
> +				      EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX);
> +			return;
> +		}
> +		new_idx = old_idx + 1;
> +	} while (!atomic_try_cmpxchg(&early_pfn_count, &old_idx, new_idx));
> +
> +	early_pfns[old_idx] = pfn;
> +}

Could this lockless array insertion race with clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs?

The count is incremented via atomic_try_cmpxchg before the PFN is actually
written to early_pfns[old_idx]. If the cleanup loop reads early_pfn_count
right after the increment but before the write completes, it might read
uninitialized memory (or 0), missing the actual allocation entirely.
